title: Correcting Tree Height Calculations in a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Given a binary tree, calculate the height of each node's subtree. However, the provided code has a logical bug causing incorrect results. You must correct this issue to pass all test cases.
statement.inputFormat:
# Each line represents a binary tree node, where each node is in the format '[value, left_child, right_child]',
# The binary tree has a maximum of 10000 nodes.
statement.outputFormat:
The height of each node in the binary tree, where the output is in the format '[height1, height2, ...]',
# The height of each node is calculated as the number of edges from the root to the node.
statement.notes: The binary tree is represented as a list of nodes, where each node contains a value and two child pointers.

examples.explanation:
For the given binary tree,
      1
   /   \
  2     3
 / \   / \
4  5 6   7
The height of the root node (1) is 0,
the height of the left subtree of 2 is 2 (2 -> 3 -> 4),
and the height of the right subtree of 2 is 1 (2 -> 3 -> 6).

hints.text: Review the binary tree height calculation logic in the code to identify the logical bug.

editorial.title: Correcting Binary Tree Height Calculations
editorial.intuition: A recursive function is needed to calculate the height of each node's subtree. A binary tree height calculation function should recursively call itself on the left and right child nodes, incrementing the height by 1 for each recursive call.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution involves a nested loop to check every node in the binary tree. This will have a higher time complexity compared to the recursive solution.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a recursive DFS to traverse the binary tree, updating the height of each node as you go.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly calculating the height of the root node
Forgetting to update the height of child nodes
